coredata.dc:title: Selective fluoride detection based on modulation of red emissive carbon dots fluorescence by zirconium-alizarin complex: Application to Nile River water and human saliva samples
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Yahya S.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: Fluoride is an essential ion for dental health that is optimally supplemented through drinking water, toothpastes, and other oral hygiene products. This work reports a selective, simple, and inexpensive fluorescent sesnor for fluoride quantification. The sensor exploits the red fluorescence of carbon dots coupled with the colorimetric response of a zirconium-alizarin complex. The zirconium-alizarin exhibits an absorption band at 530 nm that overlaps the carbon dot excitation peak, resulting in inner filter effect-based quenching of the 612 nm red emission. However, in the presence of fluoride, binding disrupts the zirconium complex, releasing free alizarin with an absorption blue shifted to 420 nm. This reduces spectral overlap and restores carbon dot fluorescence. The carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and exhibited excellent fluorescence quantum yield (16.3 %). Structural and optical characterizations were performed using TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, UV–vis, and fluorescence techniques. Under optimized conditions, the probe provided selective turn-on fluorescence detection of fluoride with a linear response from 1.0 to 130.0 μM and limit of detection (S/N = 3) of 0.32 μM. Practical utility was demonstrated by accurate quantification of fluoride in Nile River water and human saliva samples. This dual nanoprobe strategy offers a rapid and cost-effective platform for fluoride analysis in support of healthcare and environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluoride
Red-emissive carbon dots
Zirconium-alizarin complex
Inner-filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluoridedetection using the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az sensing system
Analysis of fluoridecontent in Nile River water samples
Analysis of fluoride content in saliva samples
Selectivity of N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system towards fluoride ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the Research Priorities and Najran Research funding program. (NU/NRP/MRC/12/14).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (inset is size distribution histogram), (B) FTIR spectrum, and (C) XRD of N@R-CDs.
XPS spectrum of N@R-CDs. (A) Full scan spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum and (D) O1s spectrum.
(A) UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of N@R-CDs. (B) Fluorescence spectrum of N@R-CDs excited from 520 to 590 nm.
(A) The effect of variation of concentrations of fluoride (0.0–130.0 μM) on the emission intensity of N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system. (B) Relationship of (F/F0) against concentration of fluoride.
Schematic presentation of fluoride determination using N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system.
Comparison of this work with other reported fluorescent probes for the detection of fluoride.

row.entry: Human saliva and Nile River water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the analysis of real water samples and human saliva samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Selective fluoride detection based on modulation of red emissive carbon dots fluorescence by zirconium-alizarin complex: Application to Nile River water and human saliva samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluoride is an essential ion for dental health that is optimally supplemented through drinking water, toothpastes, and other oral hygiene products. This work reports a selective, simple, and inexpensive fluorescent sesnor for fluoride quantification. The sensor exploits the red fluorescence of carbon dots coupled with the colorimetric response of a zirconium-alizarin complex. The zirconium-alizarin exhibits an absorption band at 530 nm that overlaps the carbon dot excitation peak, resulting in inner filter effect-based quenching of the 612 nm red emission. However, in the presence of fluoride, binding disrupts the zirconium complex, releasing free alizarin with an absorption blue shifted to 420 nm. This reduces spectral overlap and restores carbon dot fluorescence. The carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and exhibited excellent fluorescence quantum yield (16.3 %). Structural and optical characterizations were performed using TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, UV–vis, and fluorescence techniques. Under optimized conditions, the probe provided selective turn-on fluorescence detection of fluoride with a linear response from 1.0 to 130.0 μM and limit of detection (S/N = 3) of 0.32 μM. Practical utility was demonstrated by accurate quantification of fluoride in Nile River water and human saliva samples. This dual nanoprobe strategy offers a rapid and cost-effective platform for fluoride analysis in support of healthcare and environmental monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride is an essential trace element required for the normal mineralization of bones and formation of dental enamel [1]. Optimal fluoride exposure prevents tooth decay and can even reverse early dental caries [2]. For these reasons, fluoride has become an important additive in toothpaste and mouth rinses. The World Health Organization recommends an optimal fluoride concentration in drinking water of 1.5 mg/L [3]. At this level, fluoride provides protection against dental cavities while avoiding the risk of detrimental health effects. Exposure to drinking water containing less than 0.5 mg/L fluoride can lead to a higher incidence of dental caries, especially in children [3]. However, excessive fluoride intake from water sources containing more than 1.5 mg/L carries health risks. Prolonged exposure to higher fluoride levels can cause dental fluorosis, characterized by white spots and streaking on teeth [4]. In severe cases over many years, skeletal fluorosis affecting bone structure may occur. High fluoride can also negatively impact childhood cognitive development [5]. Therefore, maintaining fluoride at optimal recommended levels in drinking water provides essential protection for dental health without the adverse effects posed by deficiency or excess. Toothpastes represent an important source of fluoride for dental health and are constantly being reformulated by manufacturers seeking to improve caries prevention [6]. The fluoride present in toothpaste helps protect both baby and permanent teeth against decay. Fluoridated toothpastes have proven effective for dental caries control and provide the most cost-efficient means of administering fluoride. The typical concentration found in commercial toothpastes ranges from 1000 to 1100 mg/L fluoride [7]. Salivary fluoride levels have been correlated with the efficacy of cavity prevention [8]. Studies have demonstrated an inverse relationship exists between the fluoride content in whole saliva and the incidence of dental caries [8].

Traditional analytical techniques for fluoride quantification like potentiometry [9], colorimetry [10], and spectrophotometry [11,12] are hampered by significant limitations. Potentiometric measurements with fluoride ion-selective electrodes lack precision and require extensive conditioning [13]. Colorimetric and spectrophotometric methods involve lengthy and multi-step sample processing [14]. While other approaches such as ion chromatography [15,16] and voltammetric sensors [17,18] that can determine fluoride levels, rely on expensive equipment and complex sample preparation. In contrast, fluorescent methods provide a rapid, straightforward, and highly sensitive technique for fluoride detection in diverse samples [19–27]. Fluorescence methods can selectively detect fluoride in complex matrices without extensive pretreatment. Their exceptional sensitivity meets requirements even at trace fluoride levels. Fluorometric probes are ideally suited for point-of-use and field analysis applications where ease of operation and rapid response are critical. Given these advantages, fluorescence-based sensors offer an attractive new platform for practical fluoride detection that can overcome the significant limitations of other analytical techniques. While fluorescent probes offer many advantages for fluoride sensing compared to traditional techniques, the fluorometric methods described in earlier reports have some limitations. Pyrene-based sensors face potential interference from similar anions like cyanide and are limited in aqueous solubility, restricting their application in biological samples [28,29]. Coumarin-based sensors, while specific for fluoride, may respond to other anions and exhibit diminished performance in complex real samples [30,31]. The synthesis of imidazole-based sensors often involves multiple steps, and their response and selectivity are highly dependent on substituents, with potential stability issues in aqueous samples [32,33]. Fluorescein-based sensors require prior hydrolysis of the scaffold, limiting their applications [19], while benzothiadiazole-based sensors are mainly restricted to organic solvent use and face challenges with selectivity [21]. Anthracene-based sensors are prone to photobleaching, affecting stability, and exhibit modest selectivity with responses to various anions [22]. Imine-based sensors experience limited aqueous stability due to reversible Schiff base formation, and their selectivity relies heavily on substituents [34]. For BODIPY-based sensors, the synthesis often requires multiple steps and strict anhydrous conditions which can make scale-up and practical applications more challenging [35]. Naphthalene-based sensors may have limited water solubility and sensitivity, restricting their use to organic solvents and requiring high analyte concentrations for detection in real samples [36]. Urea-based sensors exhibit a narrow linear range and modest selectivity with responses to various anions, with limited demonstrated applications [37]. The use of metallic nanoparticles (NPs) such as CuNCs [38,39], AuNPs [40], and AgNPs [41] in sensing fluoride ions faces limitations due to their potential toxicity, variable stability in aqueous solutions, complex and costly synthesis processes, and challenges in achieving selective detection in complex mixtures. Conversely, carbon dots (CDs) present significant advantages, including excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, high aqueous stability, cost-effective and straightforward synthesis, and superior fluorescence properties [42,43]. CDs offer enhanced selectivity and sensitivity for target molecules, making them a preferable alternative for safer, more reliable, and efficient sensing applications [44–46].

CDs are a class of nanomaterials consisting of carbon-based nanoparticles with sizes typically ranging from a few to several tens of nanometers [47]. They possess unique optical properties, such as strong fluorescence and tunable emission wavelengths, making them attractive for various applications in fields such as sensing, imaging, bioimaging, and optoelectronics [48–51]. The versatility of carbon dots arises from their diverse range of applications [52]. Additionally, carbon dots have found uses in sensing applications, including detection of heavy metals, ions, organic compounds, and biomolecules [52]. Their exceptional optical properties and surface functionalization potential make them ideal candidates for advanced sensor development. One of the key advantages of carbon dots is their low cost and ease of synthesis [47]. Various methods have been developed for the preparation of carbon dots, including hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and pyrolysis [53]. Among these methods, hydrothermal synthesis is particularly noteworthy due to its simplicity and scalability. With this approach, carbon dots can be readily prepared using a wide range of carbon sources, such as organic compounds, polymers, or biomass [53]. In our study, we focused on the preparation of red emission carbon dots using hydrothermal methods. Specifically, we utilized semicarbazide and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the precursors.

This work successfully demonstrated a novel fluorescence-based strategy for selective fluoride detection using nitrogen-doped red emission carbon dots (N@R-CDs) coupled with a zirconium-alizarin (Zr-Az) colored complex. The approach relies on disruption of the zirconium complex by fluoride, eliminating inner filter effects that quench N@R-CDs fluorescence. Under optimized conditions, the system provided rapid, simple, and highly sensitive quantification of fluoride across a wide linear range down to nanomolar levels. Practical applicability was shown through excellent recovery values of fluoride in saliva samples collected after brushing with fluoridated toothpaste. Recovery of spiked fluoride from Nile water samples further validated the accuracy. Monitoring fluoride levels in human saliva and River water is crucial for assessing oral health and environmental safety, respectively, as fluoride plays a significant role in dental care and can impact overall water quality.

Fluorescence spectra were acquired using a Shimadzu fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1601, Japan) employing a 1-cm path length quartz cuvette. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet 6700 Spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) using the KBr pellet technique across the 400–4000 cm−1 range. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurements were conducted on a Philips PW 1700 diffractometer (Eindhoven, Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using a PerkinElmer PHI-5400 photoelectron spectrometer. Zeta size analysis was carried out with a Malvern Nano ZS90 instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a JEM-1400 microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operating at a 200 KV acceleration voltage.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5 %) and sodium fluoride (NaF, analytical grade) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), semicarbazide, sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Alizarin Red S and zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4) were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Double distilled water was produced using an Aquatron Automatic Water Still (Model 4L/Hr) and had a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Carbon dots were prepared via a hydrothermal approach using 0.5 g of semicarbazide and 1.0 g of OPD as precursors. The precursors were dissolved in 30 mL of double distilled water and transferred to a 100 mL autoclave. The mixture was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the yellow supernatant containing crude carbon dots. This supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and then dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h using a 1000 molecular weight cutoff dialysis membrane to remove unreacted precursors and impurities. Finally, the purified dialyzed product was frozen and lyophilized to obtain the carbon dot powder.
The procedures and calculations for quantum yield are provided in the Supplementary Data file.

The zirconium alizarin complex was prepared by mixing 5.0 mL of 50 mg/mL of Alizarin Red S and 5.0 mL of 50 mg/mL ZrCl4 solution. The solution was then stirred for 30 min to ensure complete complexation between Zr4+ ions and Alizarin Red S. The final volume of the solution was adjusted to 100 mL by adding double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluoridedetection using the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride detection was performed by first preparing a blank solution containing 0.5 mL of the zirconium alizarin complex added to 5 mL of N@R-CDs and diluted to 10 mL total volume with phosphate buffer (pH 5.0) in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask. The fluorescence of this blank was measured at an excitation wavelength of 565 nm and emission wavelength of 612 nm. For the fluoride samples, 0.5 mL of the zirconium complex was again added to 5 mL of N@R-CDs, followed by addition of 0.5 mL of varying fluoride concentrations. The solutions were diluted to 10 mL total volume with phosphate buffer (pH 5.0) to obtain final fluoride concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 130.0 μM. After allowing reaction between the fluoride and zirconium ions for 5.0 min, the fluorescence of these solutions was measured under identical conditions to the blank (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluoridecontent in Nile River water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the fluorescent probe for Nile River water samples, water specimens were collected from the Ibrahimiya Canal in Assiut Province, Egypt. The water samples were processed prior to fluoride analysis by first centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatants were filtered using 0.22 μm syringe filters and appropriately diluted with deionized water. These pretreated environmental samples were then spiked with 0, 3, 10 and 20 μM of fluoride standard and analyzed using optimized detection procedures (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluoride content in saliva samples
ce:section.ce:para: Saliva samples were obtained from three healthy volunteers to assess the ability of the fluorescent probe to quantify fluoride levels. The volunteers first brushed their teeth for 5 min using a standard toothpaste containing 1500 mg/L of fluoride. Saliva was then collected by having participants place a sterile gauze pad in their mouth for 2 min. This was repeated 5 min after brushing. The saliva-containing gauze pads were squeezed into plastic tubes using 5 mL syringes, providing either fresh samples for immediate analysis or frozen samples for later use [54]. For analysis, 1 mL of saliva was diluted to 5 mL total volume with double distilled water followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5.0 min. The clear supernatant was collected and further diluted to 10 mL total volume [8]. These prepared saliva solutions were then analyzed using the standard fluorescence detection procedures developed previously (Scheme 1).

The shape and size of the N@R-CDs were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The particles appeared spherical and well-distributed, as depicted in Fig. 1 A. The TEM image showed that the N@R-CDs were consistently sized, with an average diameter of around 3.6 nm (inset, Fig. 1A).

The FTIR spectrum of the N@R-CDs synthesized displays characteristic peaks indicative of the structural components (Fig. 1B). The peaks at 3135 cm−1 and 3030 cm−1 suggest the presence of primary and secondary amines. A shoulder peak at 2800 cm−1 signifies aliphatic C-H stretches, indicating the existence of hydrocarbon chains [55,56]. The sharp peak at 1738 cm−1 suggests the presence of carbonyl groups (CO). Peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1487 cm−1 indicate CC and C-N stretching vibrations within aromatic rings. Peaks at 1270 cm−1 and 1201 cm−1 may suggest C-N stretching vibrations in aliphatic amines or heterocyclic structures formed during cyclization. The peak at 1096 cm−1 indicates C-N bending vibrations, further supporting nitrogen incorporation within the dot structures. The peak at 963 cm−1 could be associated with C-O stretching. The peaks at 740 cm−1, 600 cm−1, and 509 cm−1 may relate to C-H bending vibrations in aromatic rings or other cyclic structures [57,58].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum (Fig. 1C) indicates that the N@R-CDs exhibited a broad peak centered at approximately 21.8°. This peak corresponds to the diffraction pattern of the (002) lattice of graphitic carbon [59].

XPS analysis was performed to investigate the elemental composition and confirm the presence of surface functional groups in the N@R-CDs. The full scan XPS spectra of the N@R-CDs (Fig. 2 A) displayed peaks at 284.58 eV, 399.71 eV, and 529.46 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. In the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2B), three distinct peaks at 284.68 eV, 285.46 eV, and 288.31 eV were assigned to C-C, C-N/C-O, and CO, respectively. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2C) exhibited three peaks at 399, 399.5, and 400.2 eV, attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and amino N, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) revealed two peaks at 529.42 eV, and 530.91 eV, corresponding to C-O and CO, respectively [60,61].

Further spectroscopic characterization of the synthesized N@R-CDs was carried out using UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometry and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibited three distinct features including a shoulder peak at 225 nm attributed to π-π* transitions of CC bonds, a peak at 277 nm corresponding to n-π* transitions of non-bonding electrons, and a broad small peak at 422 nm indicating allowed electronic transitions in the carbon dot network (Fig. 3 A) [62]. Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra were measured by varying the excitation wavelength from 520 to 590 nm (Fig. 3B). The maximum emission intensity peak occurred at 612 nm when using an excitation wavelength of 565 nm. Notably, the emission peak position remained constant as the excitation wavelength changed, demonstrating excitation-independent fluorescence behavior typical of carbon dots. The fluorescence quantum yield of the N@R-CDs was calculated to be 16.3 % relative to quinine sulfate standard, indicating excellent fluorescence efficiency.

The fluorescence stability of the N@R-CDs was verified by evaluating the effects of varying pH values, ionic strength, UV-irradiation time, and temperature on their fluorescence intensity. Across the pH range from 2.0 to 11.0, the emission of the N@R-CDs was highest between pH 7 and 9, with some decrease in very acidic or very basic conditions (Fig. S1A). This suggests the carboxyl groups on the dot surface can become protonated under acidic environments, increasing aggregation which diminishes fluorescence [63]. Testing different sodium chloride concentrations showed minimal impact on fluorescence intensity, indicating the N@R-CDs have good tolerance to ionic strength changes (Fig. S1B). Continuous irradiation for six hours did not significantly reduce emission, demonstrating excellent photostability (Fig. S1C). Elevating the temperature resulted in some decline in fluorescence, likely due to thermal quenching effects (Fig. S1D).

While the highest F/F0 ratio was observed at pH 7, a pH of 5 was chosen for the remainder of the study due to several factors (Fig. S2A). In acidic conditions below pH 5, the alizarin dye exists primarily in its yellow protonated form. This provides greater color contrast upon fluoride addition which displaces the dye from the zirconium complex, restoring the yellow color [64,65]. Conversely, at pH above 5.0 the free alizarin exists in its red deprotonated form, which remains overlapped with the N@CD excitation spectrum even after fluoride addition, producing only a small change in fluorescence intensity. While the N@CDs may exhibit better stability in neutral or basic conditions, the colorimetric distinction between the red complex and yellow free dye is reduced. Therefore, pH 5.0 represents the best compromise, where free alizarin is sufficiently yellow for colorimetric sensing, while N@CD stability is maintained as much as possible, even though it is slightly below the optimal carbon dot pH condition. Complexation time was studied from 1 to 30 min, with 5.0 min determined to be sufficient for fluoride binding (Fig. S2B). An incubation time of 5.0 min resulted in the maximum F/F0 ratio, with longer incubation times up to 30 min maintaining a similar constant ratio. The amount of zirconium alizarin reagent was tested from 0.1 to 1 mL, with 0.5 mL found optimal to balance adequate fluorescence ratio (F/F0).

Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence response of the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system was evaluated across a fluoride concentration range of 1.0 to 130.0 μM. As the fluoride ion concentration increased (Fig. 4 A), the red fluorescence of the N@R-CDs at 612 nm progressively recovered due to disruption of the zirconium complex and associated inner filter effects. A linear relationship (R2 = 0.9953) between the relative fluorescence change and fluoride concentration was obtained (Fig. 4B). The calibration curve could be described by the equation F/F0 = 0.012 × CFluoride – 1.13. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.32 μM based on the standard deviation of blank signals, which is well below the 32.6 μM regulatory limit for fluoride in drinking water. This sensing strategy provides rapid, simple, and sensitive quantification of fluoride without requiring any modifications or processing steps. Additionally, the method offers a broad linear range and enhanced sensitivity when compared to the majority of fluorescence-based approaches for detecting fluoride as shown in Table 1 . This facile dual probe system enables accurate and selective fluoride analysis with high analytical performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system towards fluoride ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system for fluoride detection was evaluated by testing a panel of other potentially interfering anions. A variety of inorganic anions including chloride, bromide, iodide, acetate, nitrate, carbonate, sulfate, phosphate, bicarbonate, nitrite, and cyanide were examined, all at high 10.0 mM concentrations (Fig. S3A). Negligible changes in fluorescence intensity were observed upon addition of any of these competing anions to the probe solution. In contrast, introduction of fluoride ions (70 μM) caused a dramatic enhancement in fluorescence. These results demonstrate the highly selective response of this sensing system to fluoride over other common anions. Even at very high concentrations, these other species did not interfere with fluoride detection. The selectivity arises from the unique ability of fluoride to disrupt the zirconium-alizarin complex and relieve the inner filter effect quenching. The carbon dot fluorescence at 612 nm remained unaffected by the interfering anions. The selectivity of the zirconium-alizarin complex for fluoride forms the basis of the sensing mechanism. The hard Lewis acidic Zr4+ metal center exhibits a strong affinity for the hard Lewis basic fluoride anion. Fluoride can readily disrupt and bind to the zirconium complex, displacing the alizarin ligand [76]. Other anions like chloride, bromide, sulfate and phosphate have weaker interactions with the metal center and do not displace the colored ligand. Additionally, fluoride is a strong hydrogen bond acceptor and can interact with the phenolic hydroxyl protons of alizarin. Together, the high coordination preference of Zr4+ for fluoride along with hydrogen bonding provides selective recognition of fluoride over other anions. In addition to the anions tested, the selectivity of the probe was also investigated against potentially interfering cations including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+. These cations were examined at 1.0 μM concentration and, importantly, no noticeable changes in fluorescence intensity were observed upon introduction to the nanoprobe system (Fig. S3B). The CDs' fluorescence remained unaffected, and the addition of these cations did not restore or enhance the quenched signal. The inability of the cations to displace alizarin and disrupt the zirconium complex contributes to selectivity. Moreover, the hard Lewis basic fluoride exhibits a much higher affinity for the Zr4+ center compared to the examined cations which are soft or borderline acids. Common amino acids like cysteine, arginine, glutamine, serine and leucine (1 mM) that may interfere during analysis of saliva samples showed no significant interference (change in F/F0 ratio). Additionally, 1 mM levels of carbohydrates such as glucose, fructose and amylase enzyme did not cause any discernible effect on the fluorescence response of the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system (Fig. S3C). The exceptional selectivity indicates this dual probe system is reliable for detecting fluoride in complex real world water samples with minimal interferences from background anions, cations and biological substances.

The sensing mechanism relies on the unique coordination chemistry between fluoride ion and the zirconium-alizarin complex. Zr4+ forms a stable complex with alizarin (Az) through chelation with the phenolic oxygen atoms, yielding an intense red colored species with a characteristic absorption peak at 530 nm. In our system, the N@R-CDs, which exhibit red fluorescence, and the Zr-Az complex, which has a broad absorption band centered at 530 nm, demonstrate significant spectral overlap (Fig. S4A). This overlap is crucial because when the Zr-Az complex absorbs light at the same wavelength that excites the N@R-CDs, it effectively reduces the amount of light that reaches the N@R-CDs, thereby decreasing their fluorescence through the inner filter effect. However, the addition of fluoride ion disrupts the Zr-Az coordination complex. The high affinity of the hard Lewis acidic Zr4+ center for the small hard Lewis basic fluoride ion allows fluoride to replace the alizarin ligand. This dissociation of the Zr-Az complex causes the absorption band at 530 nm to blue shift to 420 nm with a decrease in intensity, reducing the spectral overlap with the CDs' excitation. Consequently, the detrimental inner filter effects are minimized, restoring the red fluorescence of the N@R-CDs (Fig. S4B).

To further support the mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted (Fig. S4C). The negligible change in fluorescence lifetimes of the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az system before and after the addition of fluoride ion (6.46 ns vs. 6.42 ns) reinforces that the quenching and subsequent fluorescence restoration are due to the inner filter effect rather than Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). FRET would have resulted in a significant change in the fluorescence lifetime due to energy transfer between the donor and acceptor molecules. The specificity of the interaction between fluoride ions and the Zr-Az complex, leading to a predictable and measurable change in the system's photophysical properties, underpins the selectivity of this method for fluoride ion detection.

The precision within the same day (Intra-day) was checked by performing three repeated tests at three varying concentrations (3.0, 10.0, and 20.0 μM) on a single day. The precision across different days (Inter-day) was measured by conducting tests at the same concentration levels over three successive days. The results, including the standard deviation (SD) and the relative standard deviation (RSD), are shown in Table S1. RSD values fewer than 2 % are considered to show very good precision, which confirms that the method is both reliable and consistent.

The accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated by performing standard addition experiments using Nile River water samples. Unspiked Nile water was first tested to determine background fluoride levels. Separate Nile water aliquots were then spiked with 3.0 μM, 10.0 μM and 20.0 μM fluoride standards and analyzed (Table 2 ). The excellent recoveries across three concentration levels in Nile River water samples demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of this dual fluorescence probe strategy. The method provides selective quantification of fluoride in complex real-world water matrices without extensive pretreatment, confirming its practical applicability.

To evaluate the accuracy of the fluorescent carbon dot method for fluoride analysis in saliva. The concentrations of fluoride in samples from the three healthy volunteers were found to be 40.21 μM, 37.52 μM, and 36.93 μM. Recovery studies were performed using saliva samples from one volunteer spiked with additional fluoride. Saliva was analyzed directly to determine baseline fluoride content. Then saliva was spiked with 3.0 μM, 10.0 μM and 20.0 μM fluoride standard and analyzed (Table 2). The excellent recovery across three concentration levels demonstrates the accuracy of the developed sensor for quantitation of fluoride in human saliva samples.

The outcomes are consistent with those achieved through the reported voltammetric method [18], indicating that the N@R-CDs/Zr-Az sensing platform provides reliable accuracy and precision for the quantitative determination of fluoride ions in samples of river water and human saliva.

In this work, a novel fluorescence-based nanoprobe for selective fluoride detection was successfully developed using carbon dots coupled to a zirconium-alizarin complex. The carbon dots provided a sensitive fluorescent signal that was modulated by inner filter effects induced by the colorimetric zirconium-alizarin species. Binding of fluoride disrupted the zirconium complex, eliminating the inner filter effect and restoring carbon dot fluorescence for a simple yet effective “turn on” sensing mechanism. The red-emitting carbon dots were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach and exhibited excellent optical properties. The sensor demonstrated rapid and cost-effective quantification of fluoride with high selectivity. Practical applicability for healthcare and environmental monitoring was shown through accurate analysis of fluoride content in saliva and Nile River water samples. This unique dual nanoprobe strategy provides a promising platform for fluoride detection to support dental health and water quality assessment.

Yahya S. Alqahtani: Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the Research Priorities and Najran Research funding program. (NU/NRP/MRC/12/14).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110184.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
